title: 2026/03/04 - GA
displayTitle: 2026-03-03 - Governance and Administration Committee - Public Service Commission, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025
topic: Public Service Commission, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025
description: Wednesday 3 March 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee Public Service Commission, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025 Public Service Commission - Sir Brian Roche, Public Service Commissioner -- Hugo Vitalis, Deputy Chief Executive – Strategy, Policy & Integrity - Callum Butler, Manager, Policy – General Public Administration
descriptionSummary: Public Service Commission, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025. Public Service Commission. Sir Brian Roche, Public Service Commissioner

chunks.text: Good morning. Welcome back to the, uh, governance and Administration Committee. Um, it's great to have you here and we've got a relatively decent amount of time to go through quite a substantive report that you've produced for us, uh, and covers some quite important themes and insights. So, uh, we'll give you, I think it would actually be quite good if you would be able to kind of go through some of the Yeah.

Key areas in, in thinking behind Yeah. The report. And then we will move to some questions from committee members over to you. Okay. Thank you. Um, thanks for the opportunity to speak to the long term insights. Briefly, I just introduced on my right, I have Hugo Vital, who is the deputy secretary responsible, uh, for this.

And Carl Butler, who worked, the three of us, worked to get this document produced. So just some preliminary comments about, you know, why this topic. I, I think from our perspective, um, integrity as a principle is under challenge. It's under challenge globally, and it's under challenge in New Zealand.

And we thought it appropriate to reflect on those challenges and how we reorientate and reposition our current system and processes to make sure that it is strengthened in the face of those challenges. Um, I think integrity is at the heart of what we do.

Um, it is the thing that allows the public to have confidence and trust in us, and so it's continuing to evolve. We do have some, we have a piece of paper we could hand around, if that's useful for you, which provides the structure of what we're going to talk to.

Yeah, it would be quite useful actually. And she goes, just gonna step us through and, and chair, if you just want to maybe just go through section by section and then you can ask the questions that you, um, yeah, that, I think that would, that Okay. Helpful. Thank you. Thank you. Uh, thank you. Hopefully Haven. We got enough copies.

Should, There's only one page. Just one. One. I was actually had some, um, actually had some technological problems. Well, I decided yesterday not to do the screen. That's, sorry. Here you go. Uh, yes. Thank you. Um, I mean, we, uh, have been, as the commission have been, uh, concerned about international trends, actually going back to sort of the mid, uh, 20 15, 20 16, uh, that sat behind the work that we did on the Public Service Act and the need to, uh, put the public service principles, uh, and the values into legislation so that, uh,

it was a clear articulation of the role of the public service and some of the core principles and values that sat behind what the public service was about. Uh, I think the long-term insights briefing, uh, we, we do this sort of scanning as a matter of course.

Uh, this is part of the work that we do. Um, long term insights briefing, um, was an opportunity for us to, uh, work through some of the, uh, uh, challenges and opportunities, uh, that faced, uh, new, uh, sort of New Zealand and the public service.

Just very quickly, uh, the topic, uh, for the briefing, uh, was publicly consulted on, which was part of the requirements on the Public Service Act. Uh, we got public submissions, uh, and, uh, this topic had, uh, overall the, the most, uh, responses for us to, um, take forward.

Uh, and, uh, we also consulted on the draft briefing. Uh, so we got input from the public and we also got input from academics. It is a small, uh, group. We did get more than we did last time. Uh, but there were some insightful things from there. Just very quickly. Uh, I mean, I think, um, as, as outlined here, uh, we see sort of some trends and challenges emerging.

Uh, so clearly, uh, social trends are quite sort of topical. Uh, uh, some of the sort of technology challenges. The, uh, social, uh, polarization, I think is covered in the, in the briefing, um, and demographic change. Uh, and, and of course we've got, uh, through, uh, increased, um, activity across borders, uh, and, uh, technology change, uh, the challenges of, um, serious organized crime as well.

Technologically, of course, there's massive changes, uh, with the emergence of ai and of course how we do that in a safe and ethical way. Uh, economic, uh, and fiscal pressures, uh, across the globe, uh, being faced. Uh, and of course the sort of global international environment is, um, quite dynamic and challenging and unpredictable.

And, uh, for a long time we've started to see, um, sort of challenges in the, the political space, polarization challenges to, uh, electoral integrity, uh, and, um, you know, geopolitical, uh, shifts. Uh, so, which are all part of our context.

So I might just stop there if, uh, or I can carry on. Yeah. I suppose, um, one of the first questions, um, one of the first questions I had, uh, around, and, and I think one of the first sections you cover is the New Zealand's position in relation to other comparable countries and our, our, and our traditional, um, position as, uh, one of the world leaders in transparency.

And we saw in 2024, the first time a slight dip in that, um, ranking. Mm-hmm. And I think, obviously there's like around something like 30 recommendations in here, but what, what are we kind of actively doing to address that specific, um, issue?

And from your knowledge and analysis of how those are calculated, what, what do you think is the main thing that has led to that decline? Mm-hmm. So is that, are you referring him specifically to the Transparency International? Yes. Yeah. Uh, perceptions of corruption. So it's correct that where, um, our rankings have, um, gone down from, uh, essentially first, uh, uh, to currently fourth, and it's held up at forth.

Uh, the difficulty with the Transparency International, uh, rankings is rather ironically that they're not that transparent about the methodology and the index. So the work we've started doing is working with the OECD in 2023, uh, to, uh, so we've engaged with them on their trust, uh, survey.

Uh, that trust survey, uh, has been expanded, uh, and we are just awaiting results of the most recent version of that. Uh, what that helps us do, so what we did as a result of the trust survey, which we think helps us understand the drivers of trust, uh, so as part of the 2023 survey, the OECD recommended that we add in additional questions into the Kiwi's count survey, which we've done around drivers of trust.

Um, and, uh, so we added those in and we also boosted our sample set from a thousand to 2000 every quarter. Uh, so with those changes, we're starting to get much more robust sense of the drivers of trust for New Zealanders. Uh, at the moment, the latest results are in terms of service experience, uh, 84% of New Zealanders are satisfied.

That's actually the highest ranking we've had, uh, on that series. Uh, in addition, public trust in the public service has gone up to 64%, uh, for comparison trust in the private sector, although that's increased quite significantly is 51%, so quite a bit lower. Underneath those drivers, we're getting much more data about what actually drives trust.

So those are some of the things we're doing to try and understand what the actual drivers are. The transparency surveys important, I don't wanna dismiss that because it's a perception survey, uh, and we are concerned about that. But underlying that, we're trying to get to the, to drivers to understand, uh, the impacts of what we do in terms of Kiwi's trust and confidence.

We we're also driving stronger processes within the public sector to make it really clear about it's everybody's responsibility to understand and maintain standards. And I think with the recent release of the code of conduct, um, stronger reporting and measurement of things that go wrong, it gives us a better insight.

And we do have a network of integrity champs embedded across. Um, it, these things are all important for us to understand where the mood is and where the balances, because, you know, although we have slipped, we are still the envy of many other countries that, you know.

So we are fortunate that I think integrity continues to be highly regarded within the public sector, but we are not naive to suggest that it's not under threat. So we're just trying to get the balance right. Um, but I think the committee can be assured this will continue to be very dynamic, I think over the next five to 10 years.

I think the rate of change and threat in that time will be greater than what we have experienced in recent decades. And I'd just also add, like the Transparency International, um, uh, surveys about perceptions of corruption in relation to corruption, specifically the Serious Fraud office, which was referenced in the briefing, is leading a cross government work program on anti-corruption.

Um, as part of that, they had ran a pilot, an anti-corruption tool that helps agencies to kind of self assist. And as part of our, um, fifth National Action plan with open government partnership, there's a commitment to kind of further develop that tool and look at rolling it out agencies more broadly. So, um, in terms of better understanding the corruption kind of landscape and how we can respond to that, And we do fear due to socioeconomic reasons, a number of people are more vulnerable than would otherwise be the case to literally turning the other way

and not reporting or not intervening. I'll just check. Um, I've got three questions sub to this transparency issue. No, that's A pretty broad one was not specific. It was Okay. Because is your separate as well. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Andy, can I just start with two, uh, two areas of question.

First of all, the, the value of doing the LTIB, um, given the changes which are, um, you know, uh, occurring in other legislation, and would you choose to do the LTIB because it's worth doing it? Um, as opposed to, because you have to do it.

Um, uh, I mean, you know, the, the government policy is, is clear. There will be one, um, long-term insights briefing done on behalf of the public service. That's one is required, One's required as part of our usual process. So we, we do, um, we actually as a, as a policy shop are continuously doing this sort of work.

So we, you know, for example, we're looking at, you know, the latest literature on high performing organizations. Uh, we're currently also looking at as part of this work, uh, what, uh, the best type of risk-based sort of methodology might be to take a risk-based approach, which is some of the things that the OAG and Transparency International said about the approach we take.

So we, uh, continuously scan, uh, and try and stay on top of the latest literature. So to answer your question, we, we should be doing, we should be doing this anyway, the LT a, uh, had, uh, two different aspects to it. One was public engagement.

Uh, I must admit that the public engagement on our, uh, is small because, uh, it's quite a select, uh, uh, few people who engage, uh, and also parliamentary discussion. Now. I think the single briefing, uh, will, will enable that. Yeah. Uh, as well.

Uh, but to answer your question, yes, we will do this and we always should be doing this as part of our job. Yep. Yes. I, Yeah. Just to reinforce that this is core to our business, uh, we, we would do it whether we were mandated to do it or not, because to, for us to deliver what we need to do, we have to continue to think and innovate.

Yeah. But that, that's very encouraging. And then it's, the question then is, is, um, how is that, um, that thinking, um, being made, would you make that, um, you know, disseminate that as, as this report does now? Yeah. Because, you know, it's a lot more valuable to the wider community and to parliamentarians if we can actually see it and go, ah, we, we, we see what you're thinking.

Yeah, we would, because I think we need to have a sort of informed and contemporary debate about what integrity means. Yeah. And it's evolving. And I feel certainly, um, within the core, this public sector, we, we would, we publish guidance, we talk to the guidance, we embed it, it is, it is a much more active process processing, periodically just doing a report.

Yes. You know, I, I have, as a relative newcomer, I'm still incredibly impressed by the prominence that this matter has given. And it, it's, it's just not talking. People actually need to do it, and the chief executives are leading it really well. Excellent. And look, the other part of the question I wanted to ask was about, uh, you talk quite a bit about, um, uh, the, the degree of polarization, which there is, which I think is, um, challenging for us as a society.

Uh, we seem worse in other countries, states, for example. Um, but, uh, what I'm interested in is polarization within the public sector and polarization some of the thinking polarization, some of the behavior at times. And that, that's one of those things which if people see it, they don't see the public sector as neutral, they see it as, you know, as partisan, whichever way it might be.

How do we deal with that? Are you seeing that? How do we deal with that? Um, well, so best evidence we have, um, at the moment is from the public service census. And actually the, one of the highest rating, uh, answers in relation to political neutrality, so I understand what means to be politically neutral, is that 95% across the public service.

That's a survey that's, uh, done of all public servants. That said, uh, one of the, uh, things that we are wanting to do with the Integrity Reset is to, uh, so the commission has just issued a new code of conduct. Uh, that code of conduct has both standards and guidance integrated.

Uh, the past code had a single page code and, um, 36 pages of guidance, uh, many public servants didn't know existed. Uh, alongside that, we, um, we have, it was, it was a great piece of work. It's just that times have changed and, and, um, we've integrated that work.

The second piece is we've got mandatory training. So that's, uh, so we've got two bits of mandatory training going out. The first is, uh, on our election guidance to make sure public servants understand, uh, what they can and can't do. And the second piece is on the code of conduct itself. Uh, so those are electronic modules with scenario based learning.

So we are really trying to reinforce what it means to be a public servant and serve the government of the day. We also are strengthening our induction processes to incoming public servants and training them. And then, as Hugo said, as many other professions do, you have to remain current in every two to three years, you get to do another module so that nobody can ever say they weren't aware of what the words meant or what their behaviors were expected.

And a lot of that is driven to making sure that public services are neutral. We seek the government of the day. Okay, good. How, how do you, this Supplementary? Yeah. Um, how do you manage 95% political neutrality in the public service? How do you, how do you measure that?

So, so it's a, it's a measure that's done through the public service census. Yeah. So it's a, it's a, it's an assessment of public servants themselves. Now, the second bit of that is whether they're Right. Uh, so, you know, you may, What do you mean by, Well, you may perceive, for example, that you understand political neutrality.

Yes. But in practice, uh, the, that can mean different things. So the important part of, uh, the code of conduct training, yeah. Uh, together with the Integrity Champ Champion Network. So we set up that network for the last 2023 election, that senior leaders in each agency taking responsibility for educating and training staff.

Yeah. Um, so we want to make sure that people understand, uh, essentially the, the code are the sort of professional boundaries of ethics, if you like. Uh, and, and as a profession, we look to those in order to understand and guide our behavior in order to understand what the right thing to do is.

Now, the right thing to do is not your personal opinion that's bounded within kind of those rules and processes, but there are ethical choices and, and questions about what the right thing to do is. And that's part of our training for managers as well, which is scenario based. So what percentage of the public service at the moment would be politically neutral?

Well, If your target is 95% not, no, No, that is 90. That is, it's a hundred percent. So a hundred percent of the public service right now is politically, That's the target. That's our target. Oh. And 95% are 95% s So, so what can you do about the last 5%, aside from the code of conduct train, uh, series of firstly awareness, as Hugo's pointed out, so they haven't paper what it means.

I think setting examples for them is really important so that they understand what it means in action. Um, just maintain a very high level of awareness about the need to maintain it. But human beings are human beings, you know? Um, but a hundred percent is what we would expect on any given day.

And the survey suggests that there's a gap when we look into that. Some of it is a perception, not reality, but perceptions, raw behaviors. So we've gotta strengthen process, strengthen example, and I think strengthen the behaviors that we expect. I wish you the best with your endeavors.

Other thing, that's the job. That's the, The other thing that the briefing talks about in this space as well is, is looking at kind of recruitment processes. So it's, it's something that's quite an emerging thing internationally to kind of consider what, how you might select for pro integrity sort of behaviors and attributes.

Yeah. That's the other thing that you can do to make sure that the people that you are bringing in and inducting to the public service, um, have high integrity to begin with. Thank You. These are, um, really important topics and I just wanted to double check with you. Did you have any other, did you wanna make any other comments, um, on any other areas, or are you just happy to kind of go Through this? We we're really happy.

Yeah. I mean, like you, we do think it's a really important topic, and it's, when it's breached, we have a problem, which is why we wanna safeguard it. Okay. We've got a bit of a speaking list here, but I've just final stuff on, on that issue. Obviously no one's debating the importance of that political neutrality and like my colleague, um, you know, a hundred percent is the goal that you should be reaching.

But just in, in terms of the comments around, um, recruitment and everything, obviously when you are appointing people, you can't also breach other rights, like discriminate on political views and for many low level public servants. Um, or not, it's probably not a, not the correct term, but for people that are not in very senior positions, obviously there will be, um, the ability for them to have a separate politically active private life and, and, um, a, uh, neutral, uh, professional life.

And, and how, how are you gonna make sure that those important, um, rights can exist beside each other as you go? That a hundred percent Hug might wanna kind of, it goes to the heart of what we've set out in the code of conduct. You know, you've got a day job to do and that has to be judged, but you have a private life as well.

I mean, I think that's just a boundary that the public sector and the public servants understand, but we remain really alert to it, particularly in years, like the current year where it, it, it's under pressure, but I think the guidance is really clear and the avenues of when there is a lack of clarity are very clear as well.

Okay. Okay. Tim, Thanks. And, and I'm gonna talk about a similar subject. Can I just ask one sort of supplementary on Mr. Rutherford's questions, which is, if, if the targets a hundred percent and 95% are self-identifying that they're there, I mean, it's 5% are self-identifying, they're not, which means it's probably 10% that actually aren't.

Right. Because most people like it. I'm kind of shocked that even 5% are willing to go, yeah, I'm not, I'm not Politics. It's understand, understand the concept of political neutrality, not not discharged. So you could have, So 5% don't even understand the concept. A hundred percent of public servants would say that they are politically neutral.

I believe that, but 90, but in the survey it was suggested that not all people believe even though, or that they understand what it means to be politically neutral, is what the question was. So, so, so here, here's the question I was going to ask. A couple of years ago, there was a bit of a trend called quiet quitting, but people wouldn't actually quit their job.

They would just kind of dragged their heels. Uh, a lot of people went from time to home at the time, and they could do all sorts of things. Now, back then it was a labor government. Now it's a national, so this is agnostic to who's in, in government. But, um, how do you, there's a leadership piece in here, right?

'cause how do you ensure that public service, uh, in, in your work serving the government of the day, whoever that government, whichever political parties make up that government and, and that they're actually not just delivering their outcomes and not, you know, I'm not suggesting anyone's out there saying, you should vote this party in a work capacity, or We don't like what the government are doing.

But it's that more subtle undermining kind of, I don't like this policy that the Greens have or New Zealand first have, or whoever it might be. I'm just gonna, we can just sort of quietly slow that down or drag it. You know, how do you actually make sure that you have an energized, focused public service to deliver the outcomes the government of the day want?

Yeah. That's ultimately a leadership role for every chief executive. I, I, I mean, it would be massively unprofessional to have somebody employed who wasn't committed to delivering the outcomes that have been agreed with the minister. You, you don't get to choose other than if you don't like it, you should leave. You have to work and deliver whatever's being agreed with government of the day.

That's our job without fear or favor. And I think New Zealand is a very long tradition that demonstrating that. But I don't know whether you've got, Uh, no, I'm just on the, you know, the concern about the 5%. I mean, we're talking about people who are frontline staff who work in, uh, call centers who Sure.

Uh, you know, uh, the interactions daily about being honest, respectful, responsive, those sorts of, of values, uh, and in their private lives, they're entirely entitled to their free and democratic rights. So I would say that it's not, uh, I mean, our target is, and that's part of why we're doing the code of conduct work, is to make sure that all public servants understand political neutrality at its core.

Having said that, in the, in the public service, the advisory part of the public service that does the policy advice to ministers, there's no question it would be totally unacceptable to drag your feet on a government policy. That's not, that is totally. So I, I, I get that. And I'm not doubting that, that, that anyone thinks differently.

I'm just saying the question is really about the how, how to do the public service commission and then down through the layers of public service. How do you ensure and, and what gives you confidence to go, yeah, I can see that, that these agencies or these people are, are delivering this, who, whomever is in government? Well, I, I mean, it comes back to the, as I said, the CE and ministerial relationship.

That's where, that's the where it all happens. We as the commission have the opportunity and do regularly check in with ministers about whether their programs are being delivered and any other issues that they have with the chief executive and or their leadership team. So that's quite a high touch model.

And if it's not working, then we intervene. But it's never intervened because of political neutrality. It'll be intervened because prioritizations change, that's been my experience. And we self-correct the system, self-corrects that deliver against what ministers require.

And and I appreciate you haven't been in the role, Brian, the whole time, but if I look over five years, 'cause I'm intentionally trying to cover different governments, so to to, to sort of keep it agnostic here, but how often or would you ever have to intervene? Would you ever have situations where there is a perception either from a chief executive or a minister or a senior leader that actually we are not getting the traction we should?

Maybe there's something slowing down, or, It's, to be honest, I think that relationship is, is very dynamic and very continuous. So there are times everyone's human beings have bad hair days, as they say. You know, it doesn't, the relationship doesn't always work.

We, as the commission work really hard to make sure that the relationships do work and that, you know, both parties understand each other. But, you know, we are not as, we are not there as a policeman. But if we have to be, we are, our job is to support and coach the chief executives to do the best that they can to deliver what the minister wants.

But I guess, and this will be my last question, Madam Chair, have there been instances where you've had to deal with an aspect or a part of the public service that hasn't been politically neutral? I definitely never in my time and there's never been a suggestion of a lack of political neutrality.

But Hugo, you've been around the block? No. Yeah, I'm not, I'm not aware of any instance. No. Um, okay. Uh, having, you know, political neutrality and all of these questions about what the right thing to do is are quite nuanced decisions. So they can be quite difficult to, there's no, you know, so they're always judgment calls, but, um, I've never experienced an instance where, yeah, that is based on a, you know, political view.

Now there are, you know, there is a tension in the relationship with free and frank advice. So our job is to give free and frank advice. Correct. Yeah. And, you know, once that advice is received, then the minister can say, and, uh, and they do, uh, look, thank you for that. Or, uh, you know, or sometimes less, less happy about the advice.

Uh, but then it's our job to implement, you know, we've given the advice, our job is to implement, so, and then we think about the best way to implement that. And there's no question about that. Uh, and I, you know, I work with the policy profession and, and we're all committed to doing that, to doing our best advice. Yeah. Yeah. Thanks.

Just to, um, I'll go to who's been waiting a long time for your question. Sure. But, um, just a quick supplementary on that, on, um, ministerial advice, one of the probably less good statistics in the survey results that you reported was 71% of those involved in ministerial advice felt confident that their advice was free and frank.

So that's relatively low compared to some of the other measures, um, that you had. Yep. And I think that that's really an interesting statistic because, um, it doesn't necessarily go to necessarily not being politically neutral, but it goes to providing the best advice to ministers and which is almost a step up from that.

And, and of course, as human beings, you, you want to please the, you have good relationships with the people that you work with. Mm. And so, kind of aside from assuming that that's not necessarily or entirely connected to a political view, how do you increase, um, the confidence of, um, ministerial staff to provide that free and frank advice?

'cause that's, I think most people in the public would want to see that advice being given to ministers. Mm-hmm. Yeah, I mean, one of the, uh, things, and there's actually a recent PhD on this about, um, there are different ideas of what, you know, free and frank advice actually looks like.

Uh, so, uh, we're doing work, uh, as part of the policy profession. So that's being led through DPMC to really talk through what that looks like. So, um, you know, there are, there are, um, uh, you know, so some of the survey results, and it's the first time we've, um, measured, uh, perceptions of free and frank advice, uh, are perceptions of what free and frank advice might look like.

And it's clear that that differs, uh, across the public service. Um, so one of the things is to get greater clarity about what we mean by the concept, what that looks like. Uh, and there've been sort of this active discussions about doing that. Uh, I think in some of the, you know, OIA releases, it's very clear that Free and Frank and Vice is alive and well.

Mm-hmm. Um, you know, there's quite a lot of documented evidence of that. Uh, so I don't think I, you know, we, we are quite committed 'cause it's a central constitutional principle of public service to really lifting the bar on that. Um, but having a measure is really helpful as a way of thinking through how can we get better. Yeah, It'd be really interesting to see how that, um, hopefully improves in the future Too. But thank you Madam Chair. Um, and thank you for the report.

It feels to me like, uh, one of the chief risks in terms of managing trust is the governance of procurement across the public sector. Um, we do have the SFO, but we've seen them take a near $1 million cut in their budget, uh, from 2025 to 2026.

Um, they say on repeat that their job isn't really to be that blanket coverage or to monitor. Um, we've got the OAG reports, which occasionally flag issues, um, in more of a naming and shaming way. And then we have some cases that are taken to the courts. But my observation is there's quite a high bar threshold, um, of what's deemed to be an appropriate there, which is quite a contrast with the Australian system wherein, um, 2018 they put in a judicial review of government procurement act in place.

So two parts to the question. Um, firstly, what is the, um, what is the process or monitoring of procurement from government's perspective, and then the monitoring of that? Um, and is there a need for something that's, um, less soft in terms of guidelines and policies and more firm in terms of legislative change that could aid in, um, ensuring that there's sunlight over procurement?

Um, look, I, I think that one of those, some of those questions are best for the, uh, functional lead for procurement that sits in mb 'cause that's the expertise. What I can talk about though, is some of the work we've been doing to, um, off the back of what we've seen around procurement and the public service itself.

But I dunno if that gets to your, I think that wouldn't get to your question. I think we would recognize that, and this is a matter of public record, that procurement, that there have been issues on procurement, uh, particularly in local government. But that, so we remain like there's a very high level of awareness.

Secondly, we do rely as the commission on MV leading that, but each agency has an audit every year. We, we check the internal controls, we check the separation of duties, very traditional ways of making sure that no one person has the ability to hide, you know, ill-gotten gains for want of a better word.

So all of that housekeeping is in place, but we collectively feel that it's gonna continue to come under increased pressure because the stakes are getting higher. You know, that there's some social economic issues here for some of these people for making the decision.

So we, we are strengthening the processes, we are strengthening the leadership and we're strengthening the audit. Mm-hmm. But I couldn't give you a guarantee that there isn't a mischief at any point in time. And we have to minimize that to the best of our ability. I I I, I was just gonna ask if there's a view on whether the external, um, mechanisms for accountability are sufficient.

We, we think so, but we, this whole area is, as I said earlier, very dynamic. So we, we are not sitting passively thinking we are okay, because we've now got a code of conduct, or we've got the auditors. It, it is, it, it is much more dynamic than that.

So we remain as a system alert to where the gaps are in strengthening in its best we can. And I think, as Hugo said earlier, we are also, uh, I think looking at a much more risk-based approach. Not, not all risks are equal, if that makes sense. Mm-hmm. Procurement is an Achilles heel because there has cases and there will continue to be cases, and a lot of money goes through the public sector and it goes to third parties.

So we already had speaking up, which is one of the most important ways that public servants are able to speak up about wrongdoing as, as well as through the protected disclosure regime.
